revision.text:
{{Short description|Japanese go11ess}}
{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | spouse = [[Susanoo]]<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" /> | children = [[Toshigami]],<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" /> [[Ukanomitama]]<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /> | other_names = Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto<ref name="Kansai" /> | cult_center = [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] | cult_centre = [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] | father = [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /> }} '''Kamuō Ichihime''' is a Japanese go11ess.<ref>https://archive.today/20230325013742/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9164</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e4xT_ee-PRI | title=#18 Yuta Shrine | God of rich harvest also enshrined at Inari Shrine (Shrines of Japan) | website=[[YouTube]] }}</ref><ref name="History">{{Cite web |title=Kamu Ōichihime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kamu-oichihime/ |access-date=2023-11-17 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> She is a daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]].<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />
She is referenced in the [[Kojiki]] as the second wife of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],<ref name="History" /> and the aunt of his first wife [[Kushinadahime]].<ref name="History" /><!-- sources to get to Oyamatsumi --><ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /><!-- sources to get to Ashinazuchi and Tenazuchi --><ref>{{cite book |last=Fr?d?ric |first=L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC |title=Japan Encyclopedia |author2=Louis-Frédéric |last3=Roth |first3=K. |publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |series=Harvard University Press reference library |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=My Shinto: Personal Descriptions of Japanese Religion and Culture |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/~n-havens/myshinto/htmlfile/mmori02.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><!-- sources to get to Kushinadahime --><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki she and [[Susanoo]] are the parents of [[Ukanomitama]],<ref name="Chamberlain-19">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj026.htm Section XIX.—The Palace of Suga.]</ref><ref name="Chamberlain-20">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<!--Toshigami sources--><ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]].<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>
She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market go11ess<!--sources taken from shrine article--><ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>
==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}}
==References== {{reflist}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Japanese go11esses]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 5]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:5]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:Kamuo Ichihime]] [[Category:New]] }} [[en:Kamuo Ichihime]] [[sn:Kamuo Ichihime]] [[en:Kamuo Ichihime\]]

She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market go11ess<!--sources taken from shrine article--><ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<!--Toshigami sources--><ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with [[Inari Ōkami|Inari]].<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>
She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market go11ess<!--sources taken from shrine article--><ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>

{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| spouse = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" />
| children = [[Toshigami]],<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" /> [[Ukanomitama]]<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />

'''Kamuō Ichihime''' is a Japanese goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]].<ref>https://archive.today/20230325013742/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9164</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e4xT_ee-PRI | title=#18 Yuta Shrine | God of rich harvest also enshrined at Inari Shrine (Shrines of Japan) | website=[[YouTube]] }}</ref><ref name="History">{{Cite web |title=Kamu Ōichihime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kamu-oichihime/ |access-date=2023-11-17 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> She is a daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]].<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />

She is referenced in the [[Kojiki]] as the second wife of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],<ref name="History" /> and the aunt of his first wife [[Kushinadahime]].<ref name="History" /><ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /><ref>{{cite book |last=Fr?d?ric |first=L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC |title=Japan Encyclopedia |author2=Louis-Frédéric |last3=Roth |first3=K. |publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |series=Harvard University Press reference library |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=My Shinto: Personal Descriptions of Japanese Religion and Culture |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/~n-havens/myshinto/htmlfile/mmori02.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90}}</ref>

According to the Kojiki she and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] are the parents of [[Ukanomitama]],<ref name="Chamberlain-19">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj026.htm Section XIX.—The Palace of Suga.]</ref><ref name="Chamberlain-20">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]].<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>

She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]]<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>

[http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]].<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>

She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]]<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>

A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> She is a daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]].<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />

She is referenced in the [[Kojiki]] as the second wife of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],<ref name="History" /> and the aunt of his first wife [[Kushinadahime]].<ref name="History" /><ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /><ref>{{cite book |last=Fr?d?ric |first=L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC |title=Japan Encyclopedia |author2=Louis-Frédéric |last3=Roth |first3=K. |publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |series=Harvard University Press reference library |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=My Shinto: Personal Descriptions of Japanese Religion and Culture |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/~n-havens/myshinto/htmlfile/mmori02.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90}}</ref>

According to the Kojiki she and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] are the parents of [[Ukanomitama]],<ref name="Chamberlain-19">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj026.htm Section XIX.—The Palace of Suga.]</ref><ref name="Chamberlain-20">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]].<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>

She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]]<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>

{{Short description|Japanese goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]]}}

{{Infobox deity
| type = Shinto
| spouse = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" />
| children = [[Toshigami]],<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" /> [[Ukanomitama]]<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />
| other_names = Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto<ref name="Kansai" />
| cult_center = [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]]
| cult_centre = [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]]
| father = [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />
| gender = {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}
| parents = {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563}}
| consort = {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}}
| offspring = {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}}
}}
'''Kamuō Ichihime''' is a Japanese goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]].<ref>https://archive.today/20230325013742/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9164</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e4xT_ee-PRI | title=#18 Yuta Shrine | God of rich harvest also enshrined at Inari Shrine (Shrines of Japan) | website=[[YouTube]] }}</ref><ref name="History">{{Cite web |title=Kamu Ōichihime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kamu-oichihime/ |access-date=2023-11-17 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> She is a daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]].<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />

She is referenced in the [[Kojiki]] as the second wife of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],<ref name="History" /> and the aunt of his first wife [[Kushinadahime]].<ref name="History" /><ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /><ref>{{cite book |last=Fr?d?ric |first=L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC |title=Japan Encyclopedia |author2=Louis-Frédéric |last3=Roth |first3=K. |publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |series=Harvard University Press reference library |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=My Shinto: Personal Descriptions of Japanese Religion and Culture |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/~n-havens/myshinto/htmlfile/mmori02.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90}}</ref>

According to the Kojiki she and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] are the parents of [[Ukanomitama]],<ref name="Chamberlain-19">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj026.htm Section XIX.—The Palace of Suga.]</ref><ref name="Chamberlain-20">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]].<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>

She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]]<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>

==Family tree==
{{Eight generations of Izumo}}

[http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]].<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>

She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess[[Category:replace 11 with dd]]<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>

'''Kamuō Ichihime''' is a Japanese goddess.<ref>https://archive.today/20230325013742/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9164</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e4xT_ee-PRI | title=#18 Yuta Shrine | God of rich harvest also enshrined at Inari Shrine (Shrines of Japan) | website=[[YouTube]] }}</ref><ref name="History">{{Cite web |title=Kamu Ōichihime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kamu-oichihime/ |access-date=2023-11-17 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> She is a daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]].<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />

She is referenced in the [[Kojiki]] as the second wife of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],<ref name="History" /> and the aunt of his first wife [[Kushinadahime]].<ref name="History" /><ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /><ref>{{cite book |last=Fr?d?ric |first=L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC |title=Japan Encyclopedia |author2=Louis-Frédéric |last3=Roth |first3=K. |publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |series=Harvard University Press reference library |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=My Shinto: Personal Descriptions of Japanese Religion and Culture |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/~n-havens/myshinto/htmlfile/mmori02.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90}}</ref>

According to the Kojiki she and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] are the parents of [[Ukanomitama]],<ref name="Chamberlain-19">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj026.htm Section XIX.—The Palace of Suga.]</ref><ref name="Chamberlain-20">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with [[:en:Inari Ōkami|Inari]].<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>

She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | spouse = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" /> | children = [[Toshigami]],<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" /> [[Ukanomitama]]<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /> | other_names = Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto<ref name="Kansai" /> | cult_center = [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] | cult_centre = [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] | father = [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /> | gender = {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}} | parents = {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563}} | consort = {{ill|Susanoo|WD=Q272993}} | offspring = {{ill|Ukanomitama|WD=Q3080728}} }} '''Kamuō Ichihime''' is a Japanese goddess.<ref>https://archive.today/20230325013742/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9164</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e4xT_ee-PRI | title=#18 Yuta Shrine | God of rich harvest also enshrined at Inari Shrine (Shrines of Japan) | website=[[YouTube]] }}</ref><ref name="History">{{Cite web |title=Kamu Ōichihime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kamu-oichihime/ |access-date=2023-11-17 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> She is a daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]].<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />
She is referenced in the [[Kojiki]] as the second wife of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],<ref name="History" /> and the aunt of his first wife [[Kushinadahime]].<ref name="History" /><ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /><ref>{{cite book |last=Fr?d?ric |first=L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC |title=Japan Encyclopedia |author2=Louis-Frédéric |last3=Roth |first3=K. |publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |series=Harvard University Press reference library |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=My Shinto: Personal Descriptions of Japanese Religion and Culture |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/~n-havens/myshinto/htmlfile/mmori02.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki she and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] are the parents of [[Ukanomitama]],<ref name="Chamberlain-19">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj026.htm Section XIX.—The Palace of Suga.]</ref><ref name="Chamberlain-20">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}}.<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>
She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>
==Family tree== {{Eight generations of Izumo}}
==References== {{reflist}}
[[en:Kamuo Ichihime]]

[http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90}}</ref>

[http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj026.htm Section XIX.—The Palace of Suga.]</ref><ref name="Chamberlain-20">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}}.<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari}}.<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>
She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>

She is referenced in the [[Kojiki]] as the second wife of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],<ref name="History" /> and the aunt of his first wife [[Kushinadahime]].<ref name="History" /><ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /><ref>{{cite book |last=Fr?d?ric |first=L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC |title=Japan Encyclopedia |author2=Louis-Frédéric |last3=Roth |first3=K. |publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |series=Harvard University Press reference library |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=My Shinto: Personal Descriptions of Japanese Religion and Culture |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/~n-havens/myshinto/htmlfile/mmori02.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki she and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] are the parents of [[Ukanomitama]],<ref name="Chamberlain-19">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj026.htm Section XIX.—The Palace of Suga.]</ref><ref name="Chamberlain-20">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|WD=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}}.<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>
She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>

{{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | spouse = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]]<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" /> | children = [[Toshigami]],<ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local" /><ref name="kotobank-local" /> [[Ukanomitama]]<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /> | other_names = Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto<ref name="Kansai" /> | cult_center = [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] | cult_centre = [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] | father = [[Ōyamatsumi]]<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /> | gender = {{ill|female|qid=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}} | parents = {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|qid=Q386563|1=Ōyamatsumi}} | consort = {{ill|Susanoo|qid=Q272993|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}} | offspring = {{ill|Ukanomitama|qid=Q3080728|1=Ukanomitama}} }} '''Kamuō Ichihime''' is a Japanese goddess.<ref>https://archive.today/20230325013742/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9164</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e4xT_ee-PRI | title=#18 Yuta Shrine | God of rich harvest also enshrined at Inari Shrine (Shrines of Japan) | website=[[YouTube]] }}</ref><ref name="History">{{Cite web |title=Kamu Ōichihime • . A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |url=https://historyofjapan.co.uk/wiki/kamu-oichihime/ |access-date=2023-11-17 |website=. A History . . of Japan . 日本歴史 |language=en-GB}}</ref> She is a daughter of [[Ōyamatsumi]].<ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" />
She is referenced in the [[Kojiki]] as the second wife of [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],<ref name="History" /> and the aunt of his first wife [[Kushinadahime]].<ref name="History" /><ref name="Chamberlain-19" /><ref name="Chamberlain-20" /><ref>{{cite book |last=Fr?d?ric |first=L. |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC |title=Japan Encyclopedia |author2=Louis-Frédéric |last3=Roth |first3=K. |publisher=Belknap Press of Harvard University Press |year=2005 |isbn=978-0-674-01753-5 |series=Harvard University Press reference library |access-date=2020-11-21}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=My Shinto: Personal Descriptions of Japanese Religion and Culture |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/~n-havens/myshinto/htmlfile/mmori02.html |access-date=2023-10-16 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref><ref>Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj025.htm Section XVIII.—The Eight-Forked Serpent.]</ref><ref>{{cite book |last1=Philippi |first1=Donald L. |title=Kojiki |publisher=Princeton University Press |year=2015 |isbn=978-1400878000 |pages=89–90}}</ref>
According to the Kojiki she and [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] are the parents of [[Ukanomitama]],<ref name="Chamberlain-19">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj026.htm Section XIX.—The Palace of Suga.]</ref><ref name="Chamberlain-20">Chamberlain (1882). [http://sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/kj027.htm Section XX.—The August Ancestors of the Deity-Master-of-the-Great-Land.]</ref> and [[Toshigami]]<ref name="kotobank-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605234250/https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B9%B4%E7%A5%9E-450354 |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kotobank]] |language=ja}}</ref><ref name="shinmei kokugakuin-local">{{cite web |script-title=ja:大年神 |trans-title=Ōtoshi-no-kami |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://archive.today/20230605235208/http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/otoshinokami/ |archive-date=5 June 2023 |access-date=5 May 2023 |publisher=[[Kokugakuin University]] |language=ja}}</ref> who is often identified with {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}}.<ref>"'My Own Inari': Personalization of the Deity in Inari Worship." ''Japanese Journal of Religious Studies'' 23, no. 1/2 (1996): 87-88</ref>
She is also known by the name Ohtoshimioya-no-Mikoto (大歳御祖命).<ref name="Kansai">{{Cite web |date=2021-12-26 |title=The Kuruma Otoshi-jinja Shrine Okinamai Dance |url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/article/item/20014/ |access-date=2023-09-28 |website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI |language=en}}</ref> and worshipped at [[Shizuoka Sengen Shrine]] as a market goddess<ref>''Nihon 100 no Jinja'' (19885). Nihon Kotsu Kosha, Tokyo</ref><ref>Plutschow, Herbe. ''Matsuri: The Festivals of Japan''. RoutledgeCurzon (1996) {{ISBN|1-873410-63-8}}</ref>
